skills.prompt: This topic introduces equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-6.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand that chemical equilibrium occurs in reversible reactions when the forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products. Equilibrium is dynamic: reactions continue in both directions at equal rates. Equilibrium can be reached from either direction and does not mean equal concentrations. Students must recognize the characteristics of equilibrium systems: constant macroscopic properties, ongoing molecular-level changes, and the ability to be approached from either direction. Connect to Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constants, and reaction rates. AP exam note: understanding that equilibrium is dynamic, not static, is essential. Vocabulary: reversible reaction, dynamic equilibrium, forward reaction, reverse reaction, equilibrium state, constant concentrations, closed system.

This topic covers the direction of reversible reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-6.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand that reversible reactions proceed in both directions simultaneously. Initially, only the forward reaction occurs (only reactants present), but as products accumulate, the reverse reaction rate increases until equilibrium is established. The relative rates determine the direction of net change. If forward rate exceeds reverse rate, the reaction shifts toward products. Students must analyze concentration-time graphs to determine when equilibrium is reached and which direction a system is shifting. Connect to reaction rates, equilibrium constants, and Q vs. K analysis. AP exam note: interpreting concentration vs. time graphs at equilibrium is a tested skill. Vocabulary: reversible reaction, forward rate, reverse rate, net reaction, equilibrium position, concentration-time graph.

This topic covers the reaction quotient and equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand that the equilibrium constant expression K uses the law of mass action: for aA + bB <-> cC + dD, Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. Only aqueous and gaseous species appear; pure solids and liquids are excluded. Kp uses partial pressures instead of concentrations, related by Kp = Kc(RT)^(delta-n). The reaction quotient Q has the same form but uses current (non-equilibrium) concentrations. Comparing Q to K predicts direction: Q < K shifts right (toward products), Q > K shifts left (toward reactants), Q = K at equilibrium. Students must write K expressions and use Q vs. K to predict shifts. AP exam note: Q vs. K analysis is heavily tested. Vocabulary: equilibrium constant, reaction quotient, law of mass action, Kc, Kp, Q, shift, heterogeneous equilibrium.

This topic covers calculating the equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand how to calculate K from equilibrium concentration data using ICE tables (Initial, Change, Equilibrium). Given initial concentrations and one equilibrium value, students can determine all equilibrium concentrations and calculate K. The change row uses stoichiometric ratios with a common variable x. Students must set up ICE tables correctly, solve for x (sometimes requiring the quadratic formula or simplifying assumptions), and calculate K. Approximations are valid when K is very small (x is much less than initial concentration). Connect to Q vs. K analysis, Le Chatelier's principle, and equilibrium calculations. AP exam note: ICE table calculations are classic free-response problems. Vocabulary: ICE table, initial concentration, change, equilibrium concentration, stoichiometric ratio, approximation, quadratic formula.

This topic covers the magnitude of the equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.C, Unit 7, Topic 7.5). Students should understand that the value of K indicates the relative amounts of products and reactants at equilibrium. A large K (much greater than 1) means products are strongly favored; the reaction goes nearly to completion. A small K (much less than 1) means reactants are strongly favored; little product forms. K approximately equal to 1 means significant amounts of both. Students must interpret K values qualitatively and relate them to equilibrium position. The value of K depends only on temperature, not on concentrations, pressures, or catalysts. Connect to thermodynamic favorability, Le Chatelier's principle, and Gibbs free energy. AP exam note: interpreting the magnitude of K and its implications is commonly tested. Vocabulary: equilibrium constant, product-favored, reactant-favored, equilibrium position, extent of reaction, temperature dependence.

This topic covers properties of the equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.D, Unit 7, Topic 7.6). Students should understand how manipulating reactions affects K: reversing a reaction inverts K (K_reverse = 1/K_forward); multiplying a reaction by a factor n raises K to the nth power (K_new = K^n); adding reactions multiplies their K values (K_overall = K1 x K2). K is dimensionless when using activities, but in practice, units may appear depending on the expression. Students must calculate K for modified reactions using these mathematical relationships. Kp and Kc are related by Kp = Kc(RT)^(delta-n) where delta-n is the change in moles of gas. Connect to Hess's law for enthalpy, coupled equilibria, and solubility product. AP exam note: manipulating K values when combining reactions is a tested skill. Vocabulary: equilibrium constant manipulation, inverse, power, product of K values, Kp, Kc, delta-n.

This topic covers calculating equilibrium concentrations for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.E, Unit 7, Topic 7.7). Students should understand how to use ICE tables with known K values to solve for unknown equilibrium concentrations. The process: set up the ICE table with initial concentrations, express changes using stoichiometry and variable x, write the equilibrium expression, substitute, and solve. For small K values, the assumption x is much less than the initial concentration simplifies the algebra (check that x is less than 5 percent of the initial value). For larger K or when the assumption fails, the quadratic formula is needed. Students must also solve for equilibrium concentrations when the system starts at non-equilibrium conditions. Connect to Q vs. K, Le Chatelier's principle, and acid-base equilibrium. AP exam note: ICE table problems are among the most common quantitative questions. Vocabulary: ICE table, equilibrium concentration, small-x approximation, five percent rule, quadratic formula, stoichiometric change.

This topic covers representations of equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.F, Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Students should understand how to represent equilibrium at the particulate level using diagrams showing relative numbers of reactant and product molecules. At equilibrium, the ratio of products to reactants is consistent with the K value. Particulate diagrams must conserve atoms and reflect the stoichiometric ratios. Students must interpret particulate diagrams to calculate Q or K, determine if a system is at equilibrium, and predict the direction of shift. Concentration-time graphs showing all species reaching constant values indicate equilibrium. Connect to Q vs. K analysis, Le Chatelier's principle, and dynamic equilibrium. AP exam note: particulate diagram analysis of equilibrium systems is a distinctive skill. Vocabulary: particulate diagram, equilibrium representation, Q vs. K, atom conservation, concentration ratio.

This topic introduces Le Châtelier’s Principle for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-8.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.9). Students should understand that when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to partially counteract the disturbance and re-establish equilibrium. Adding reactant or removing product shifts the reaction toward products (right); adding product or removing reactant shifts toward reactants (left). Increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium in the endothermic direction; decreasing temperature shifts it in the exothermic direction. Changing pressure (by changing volume) affects gaseous equilibria: increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer gas moles. Catalysts do not shift equilibrium; they speed both directions equally. Students must predict and explain the direction of shift for various disturbances. AP exam note: Le Châtelier’s principle is one of the most heavily tested topics. Vocabulary: Le Châtelier’s principle, stress, shift, equilibrium position, concentration change, temperature change, pressure change.

skills.skill: 7.10 Reaction Quotient and Le Châtelier’s Principle
skills.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between Q and Le Châtelier’s Principle for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-8.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.10). Students should understand that Le Châtelier’s principle can be explained quantitatively using Q vs. K analysis. When a stress is applied, Q changes relative to K: adding reactant decreases Q (Q < K, shifts right); adding product increases Q (Q > K, shifts left); removing reactant increases Q; removing product decreases Q. Temperature changes actually change K itself (K increases for endothermic reactions when heated, decreases for exothermic). Volume changes affect Q for gaseous reactions with unequal moles of gas. Students must use Q vs. K to justify predicted shifts and calculate new equilibrium positions. Connect to equilibrium calculations and thermodynamics. AP exam note: connecting Q vs. K to Le Châtelier’s qualitative predictions strengthens analysis. Vocabulary: reaction quotient Q, equilibrium constant K, stress, shift direction, new equilibrium, concentration perturbation.

This topic introduces solubility equilibria for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-5.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.11). Students should understand that the dissolution of a slightly soluble ionic compound reaches an equilibrium described by the solubility product constant Ksp. For compound AB2 dissolving as AB2(s) <-> A2+(aq) + 2B-(aq), Ksp = [A2+][B-]^2. The solid does not appear in the expression. Ksp values allow calculation of molar solubility (mol/L that dissolve) and comparison of relative solubilities of compounds with the same ion ratio. Precipitation occurs when Q > Ksp (ion product exceeds solubility product). Students must write Ksp expressions, calculate molar solubility from Ksp, and predict whether precipitation occurs. Connect to equilibrium principles, Q vs. K, and ionic compounds. AP exam note: Ksp calculations and precipitation predictions are common. Vocabulary: solubility product, Ksp, molar solubility, ion product, precipitation, slightly soluble, saturated solution.

This topic covers the common-ion effect for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-5.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.12). Students should understand that adding a common ion (an ion already present in the equilibrium) to a solution of a slightly soluble salt decreases its solubility. This is an application of Le Chatelier's principle: the added common ion increases Q above Ksp, causing the equilibrium to shift left (toward precipitation). For example, adding NaCl to a saturated AgCl solution decreases AgCl solubility because [Cl-] increases. Students must calculate molar solubility in the presence of a common ion using modified ICE tables with Ksp. Connect to Le Chatelier's principle, precipitation reactions, and buffer chemistry. AP exam note: common-ion effect calculations and qualitative predictions are tested. Vocabulary: common-ion effect, molar solubility, Le Chatelier's principle, Ksp, ion product, precipitation, solubility decrease.
